text: This is a gentleman with a small right scrotal hernia that clinically is reducible. However, as you can see in this video, it is not fully reducible intra-op.
The surgeon reveals the back wall of the hernia sac, which consists of the colon, identifying it as a cecal slider hernia.
The video is sped up two times.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the colon from the hernia sac, encountering multiple attachments that hinder complete reduction.
The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, using a third instrument for assistance. A Cartier is placed in the left lower quadrant opposite the hernia site to aid in retracting the tissue.

What the third instrument does is it allows the left hand to be used much more appropriately to assist the right hand so that it's more like open surgery with your two hands performing the dissection.
The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap after unsuccessful attempts to reduce the hernia contents, aiming to facilitate the reduction of the remaining hernia sac.
The surgeon navigates between the transverse salus fascia and the pre-peritoneal fascia during the inguinal hernia repair, leaving the transverse salus fascia adjacent to the rectus muscle.
The surgeon uses a third instrument for flap retraction during scleral hernia repair, performing early dissection laterally between the peritoneum and testicular vessels, and the vas deferens.

This way you will always know where the testicular vessel is and not injure it in case the dissection becomes less hemostatic. But make every attempt to keep the dissection hemostatic so that you can see the tissue planes and see the critical structures to dissect away from the hernia sac.
And last but not least is to always follow the Ten Commandments, even in difficult scleral hernias.

The surgeon develops the peritoneal flap in the space of Retzius using blunt dissection, following the natural tissue plane. Small vessels encountered during the dissection are cauterized to maintain hemostasis.

The surgeon assesses the amount of retro-rectus fat left against the rectus muscle and decides to work more superficially if excess fat is present. The surgical approach shifts to working laterally to avoid deep cavities.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the peritoneum, ensuring that the lobular fat remains against the abdominal wall, avoiding exposure of the transverse salus fascia against the transverse abdominis.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the surgical instrument to assist in dividing several layers, aiming to stay precisely on the peritoneum, which is the ideal plane for dissection.
The surgeon continues dissecting along the peritoneum, with visible blood present from a previous tap block procedure.
The surgeon identifies and separates additional layers from the peritoneum, ensuring that blood vessels and most of the fat are removed from this anatomical guide layer.
The surgeon attempts to dissect deeper to reach the peritoneum but encounters the cecum, revealing the absence of a peritoneal sac posteriorly.

The cecum, a partly retroperitoneal structure, is observed sliding into the indirect space, revealing the absence of a peritoneal sac. The surgeon evaluates the area to identify the next best dissection plane.
The surgeon uses small blood vessels as a guide to continue dissection close to the cecum, carefully separating these vessels from the cecum as part of the hernia repair process.
The surgeon uses a third arm to assist in performing a precise dissection of the microfacial layers, coordinating movements between the left and right hands.
The surgeon carefully dissects small blood vessels away from the surrounding tissue, aiming to separate the testicular vessels from the hernia sac and its contents.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to maintain the testicular vessels against the retroperitoneum, ensuring they remain protected while dissecting near the scrotum or inguinal canal.
And I always like to move on to something easy if what I'm doing is difficult. So I'm going to move medially.

The surgeon connects the space of Bargros dissection to the space of Retsius, carefully navigating around the VAS deferens, which is positioned more posteriorly than expected, particularly around the eight to nine o'clock position.
The surgeon uses a third instrument to assist in the precise dissection of microfacial layers, coordinating the left and right hands to prevent bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon reaches the tip of the hernia and continues to search for simpler tasks to perform, maintaining focus on the hernia area.
The surgeon uses a scissor to gently push and identify tissue planes, observing subtle movements that indicate which tissue layers can be divided.
The surgeon carefully navigates the area above the hernia sac, ensuring not to inadvertently enter it, while being less concerned about the vas deferens in this region.
The surgeon navigates through the medial area of a large chronic hernia, encountering dense scar tissue that has formed at the medial corner where the hernia pivots.
The surgeon identifies a clear areolar plane and proceeds to dissect along this plane, aiming to separate the hernia contents from the adjacent testicular vessels.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally and posterior laterally, noting the delayed visual identification of the testicular vessel, which is typically seen earlier in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies a distinct fascial layer, ensuring that the testicular vessel remains undisturbed and separate from the hernia sac during dissection.

Just like in a right colectomy, if you don't see the ureter, you don't want to do anything critical that could potentially divide the ureter. So even though I'm pretty confident with the location of the testicular vessels with the plane of dissection I've taken, I will still proceed when I divide every structure as if I could be dividing the testicular vessels.

The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia, searching for the tip of the hernia sac, which remains unseen. The medial dissection is performed last due to its complexity, with the external iliac vein becoming more visible as the dissection progresses posteriorly.

The surgeon encounters a significant amount of fat in the surgical field, which is identified as a cord lipoma or extra fibro-fatty connective tissue associated with the hernia. The surgeon continues to reduce this tissue along with the hernia sac, while pointing out the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon is positioned anterior to the iliopubic tract, carefully avoiding the genital branch and femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve, while noting the potential for anatomical variations where the genital branch may travel anterior to the iliopubic tract.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis, providing a clear view of anatomical structures in the path of dissection, ensuring precise identification and avoidance of critical structures.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue around the hernia sac, pulling it to the far left or right to unstack and reveal thinner layers. This technique helps to visualize and avoid injuring critical structures such as the vas deferens or testicular vessels that may be adjacent to the hernia sac.
The surgeon uses a third instrument to provide additional retraction, enhancing the visibility of the surgical field by pulling tissue in a different direction.

The surgeon identifies the tip of the hernia sac and carefully divides tissue attached to it, ensuring to hug the hernia sac closely to avoid injuring any nearby structures such as the vas deferens or testicular vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, leaving behind a significant amount of fat. The testicular vessels are not yet visible, prompting cautious dissection to avoid potential injury. The surgeon methodically searches for the vas deferens amidst dense scar tissue, maintaining hemostasis throughout the process.

The surgeon completes the separation of the scar tissue and medial attachments from the hernia sac. The vas deferens becomes visible, confirming that the dissection was not too close to this structure.

The surgeon carefully separates a clump of fat from the hernia sac, ensuring that the testicular vessels are not within the fat. The hernia sac is then completely reduced, with the fat managed separately as if it were a core lipoma.

The surgeon parietalizes the cord, ensuring the peritoneum is separated from posterior structures. The pink bladder tissue is visible, highlighting the importance of a hemostatic technique to observe color changes in tissue layers and fat transitions.
The surgeon dissects across the midline, approximately two centimeters deep to Cooper's ligament, preparing the retropubic space for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum and bladder tissue from the posterior structures to facilitate the placement of mesh from the retropubic space to the lateral pelvic space. A distinct anatomical structure is encountered, prompting further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the obturator fat plug, marking the posterior extent of the dissection. The fat plug remains in place as the surgeon typically does not remove it.
The surgeon has created ample space around the indirect defect, preparing to clean the area further to ensure the mesh can be properly positioned in all directions.
The surgeon identifies and points out nerves in the surgical field, likely including the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve. The general branch is noted to be thin and difficult to visualize.
The surgeon identifies and removes excess fatty connective tissue or lipoma from the surgical field, ensuring the area is clear for the mesh to lay flat against the pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon identifies the lateral frontal cutaneous nerve, ensuring it remains uninjured. A visible fascial layer covers the nerve, preventing direct contact with the mesh and protecting all three nerves in the area.
The surgeon demonstrates to the fellow the presence of an additional fascial layer that remains intact to ensure nerve coverage. A sixteen by twelve right-sided anatomical progrip mesh is visible.

The surgeon positions the mesh seam approximately two centimeters behind the posterior edge of the internal ring, ensuring it aligns with Cooper's ligament. The mesh is adjusted for proper placement in the left to right direction and tapped down to conform to the curvature of the pelvis.

The surgeon positions the mesh with the seam along Cooper's ligament, two centimeters below the internal ring, ensuring proper left to right coverage and conformity to the pelvis. The mesh is unfolded rather than unrolled, with attention to preventing it from lifting when the bladder rises. The mesh provides excellent lateral and cephalad coverage.

So now there's this big wad of colon and appendix and terminalium. kind of in this bag of sack. I wasn't sure what to do so I started exploring to see if there's any bands I can cut or any way I can free up this colon from this little bag. I was going to take a scissor and start cutting things, but it just looked like this was going to be impossible. This retroperitoneal structure really didn't have an easy way for me to free up. So eventually I decided to just leave it alone and cross my fingers that this doesn't volvilize in the future.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc 180 suture to secure the peritoneum, with assistance from a third instrument to elevate the heavy colon and prevent tearing of the lower flap while aligning it with the upper flap.

The surgeon continues suturing despite losing pneumoperitoneum, running the suture back two or three times, pulling it taut, then releasing and cutting it flush to prevent the suture tail from causing a bowel obstruction.
So thank you for watching.
